0067334d5f56f1ea.title: The Digital Credentials Stack
0067334d5f56f1ea.content:
--- marp: true theme: cdpi paginate: true --- # The Digital Credentials Stack ## A national verifiable credentials rail — in weeks, not years CDPI · [Country] · 2025 --- # The problem: credentials are still paper, PDFs, and phone calls - Citizens carry physical documents that can be lost, forged, or rejected - Ministries re-verify the same record dozens of times across departments - Each new use case requires a bespoke integration — costing time and budget - No standard trust layer means no portability across borders or agencies Verifiable Credentials Digital Transformation --- # How the Digital Credentials Stack works
Three composable modules. One open standard. Deployable in weeks.
- **Issuer** — OID4VCI-compliant; any ministry can sign and publish credentials - **Holder wallet** — citizen-controlled; works on low-end Android and offline - **Verifier** — checks cryptographic proofs without phoning home to a central server - Modular design: deploy one module today, add the rest incrementally - Built on W3C VC and OpenID standards — interoperable by default OID4VCI Open Source --- # Why it matters — and what we are asking - A student's diploma verified in seconds beats a three-week attestation queue - A farmer's land record on a phone unlocks credit without a branch visit - Usage — not enrolment — is the metric: each credential issued must be accepted somewhere - CDPI advisory is pro-bono; no vendor, no product pitch — just architecture guidance - **Next step:** let us run a 12-week feasibility sprint with your anchor ministry *Start the conversation: **info@cdpi.dev*** --- ## Assumptions - Assumed audience = government CIOs and digital transformation leads - Assumed use case = national VC rail scoping / first deployment decision - To re-draft, tell me which country, ministry, or use case to centre this on.

04d1deeb02cc8adf.title: Onboard Your Use Case onto São Tomé's National VC Portal
04d1deeb02cc8adf.content:
--- marp: true theme: cdpi paginate: true --- # Onboard Your Use Case onto São Tomé's National VC Portal ## Inter-Departmental Briefing · Ministry Leads & Registry Heads CDPI · São Tomé and Príncipe · 2025 --- # The Ask - The national VC portal is live — your ministry's credential belongs on it - Three use cases are ready for onboarding: Education Certificate, Birth Certificate, Apostille Certificate - Today's decision: each ministry signs an onboarding MOU and names a technical focal point - No new infrastructure to procure — the rail is already running --- # The Outcome We Are Targeting - **200,000 citizens** can prove identity, education, and civil status digitally - Verification in **under 3 seconds** — no office visit, no paper queue - Each credential presented at least **once per enrolled holder within 6 months** - Success metric: authentications per enrolled citizen, not enrolment count Verifiable Credentials Education --- # The Gap Today - Education certificates verified manually — takes days, costs staff time - Birth certificates require in-person registry visits for every transaction - Apostille process runs through physical stamps and consular queues - Citizens in rural São Tomé and Príncipe island bear the highest friction cost - None of these credentials are currently machine-verifiable --- # Three Principles Shaping This Design - **Inclusion and scale are synonyms** — one main channel that works for every citizen, no separate paths - **Minimalism produces scale** — every extra prerequisite field cuts a slice of the population - **Measure usage, not enrolment** — a credential no one presents is not a public good --- # Proof Points - **Trinidad and Tobago** — education credentials live on a national portal; used to access job portals within weeks of launch - **Brazil** — farmer land records issued as VCs; access to credit unlocked for smallholders who previously lacked paper proof

3 seconds

Target verification time — online and offline QR path
--- # Inclusion Design — Who Gets Left Out If We Get This Wrong - **No smartphone?** — Paper QR fallback; verifier scans, citizen carries printed credential - **No connectivity?** — Offline verification path; verifier device caches trust registry - **No prior digital ID?** — Onboarding must not require digital ID as a prerequisite; civil registry record is sufficient - **Low literacy?** — Wallet UX in Portuguese, icon-led; no text-only flows - **Príncipe island residents** — batch issuance covers existing record holders before app launch Inclusion --- # Rollout Proposal - **Weeks 1–4 · Onboarding prep** — data quality audit per issuer; focal points named; MOU signed - **Weeks 5–10 · Integration** — API or batch connection from each registry to the issuance pipeline - **Weeks 11–16 · Go live** — credentials issued; verifiers trained; citizen comms launched - **Weeks 17–26 · Post-rollout support** — usage monitored weekly; pause trigger if auth rate < 10% at week 20 - **Scale trigger** — move to next use case cohort when 40% of target population holds and has presented credential --- # Exclusion Risks by Use Case | Use Case | Population | Key Risk | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | Education Certificate | School leavers + alumni | Paper records not yet digitised | Data digitisation sprint before issuance pipeline | | Birth Certificate | All 200,000 citizens | Incomplete rural registry coverage | Batch issuance from civil registry; mobile enrolment for gaps | | Apostille Certificate | Diaspora + businesses | Low in-country verifier footprint | Consular and embassy verifier onboarding in parallel | --- # The Ask — What We Need From Each Ministry Today - **Name a technical focal point** who will own the API or batch integration - **Commit to a data quality audit** within the first four weeks - **Sign the onboarding MOU** so the SP can begin integration scoping - The portal is live — the only thing that delays your citizens is this decision --- ## Assumptions - Assumed audience = inter-departmental ministry leads and registry heads, São Tomé and Príncipe - Assumed use cases = Education Certificate, Birth Certificate, Apostille Certificate; target population 200,000 - Assumed ask = each ministry onboards onto the already-deployed national VC portal - To re-draft: tell me which use case, ministry, or slide to adjust. - Rollout timeline follows the DaaS Spark Tier 16-week active + 10-week support model from the scope document.
04d1deeb02cc8adf.source: country is sao tome, audience is interdepartmental, use case is education, birth certificate, apostille certificate, population 200k, The ask is to onboard usecases onto the recently deployed govt portal

165dd860431c6dc6.title: Blog: Your National Credentials Rail Doesn't Have to Take Years
165dd860431c6dc6.content:
# Blog: Your National Credentials Rail Doesn't Have to Take Years

Somewhere in your government, a citizen is waiting three weeks for a paper certificate to be stamped, couriered, and re-verified by an office that issued the same record six months ago. That wait is not a policy failure. It's an infrastructure gap — and it's one we now know how to close quickly.

**The Digital Credentials Stack (DCS)** is a composable set of open-source modules that lets a country stand up a national verifiable credentials rail in weeks. It has three parts: an OID4VCI-compliant issuer (any ministry can use it to sign and publish credentials), a holder wallet (citizen-controlled, works on low-end Android, works offline), and a verifier (checks cryptographic proofs without calling back to a central server). Each module is independent. You deploy what you need today and add the rest incrementally.

Think of it like the container-shipping system. Before standardised containers, every port had its own crates, cranes, and loading rules. After standardisation, any ship could dock at any port. The DCS does the same for trust: once a credential is issued in a standard format, any verifier — a bank, a border post, a university admissions office — can check it instantly without a phone call or a paper trail.

**The inclusion case is the scale case.** A wallet that works offline and on a low-end device isn't a concession to the margins — it's the design that reaches everyone. Every format restriction (smartphone-only, connectivity-required) is a population you've decided not to serve.

The metric that matters isn't how many credentials you issue. It's how many times those credentials are *used* — authentications per holder, acceptance points per use case, time-to-verify versus the paper baseline.

CDPI's advisory is pro-bono. We don't sell products or recommend vendors. We help governments ask the right architecture questions before they commit.

Curious whether the DCS fits your country's context? Let's talk.

*Read more or start a conversation: **info@cdpi.dev***

27644a48fa01e3b9.title: Technical Advisory Note — Verifiable Credentials Infrastructure for Seychelles
27644a48fa01e3b9.content:
# Technical Advisory Note — Verifiable Credentials Infrastructure for Seychelles

**Ask:** How should Seychelles architect a standards-based verifiable credentials layer?

**Why:** Citizens carry paper proofs today; VC issuance cuts re-verification cost and time.

**Scope:** VC issuance, presentation, and trust infrastructure for Seychelles government services.

**Context:**
- Seychelles has a small, high-connectivity population of roughly 100,000 citizens.
- No confirmed national VC layer exists; civil registry is the logical anchor issuer.
- Cross-border use cases (employment, travel, education) are high-priority for an island economy.

---

## Options

| Dimension | W3C VC 2.0 + SD-JWT VC | W3C VC 2.0 + JSON-LD + BBS+ | ISO/IEC 18013-5 mDL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Selective disclosure** | Native (SD-JWT salted hashes) | Native (BBS+ unlinkable proofs) | Native (mdoc DeviceResponse) |
| **Offline verification** | Requires pre-cached issuer keys | Requires pre-cached issuer keys | Natively designed for offline |
| **Cross-border readiness** | High — OIDC4VP widely adopted | Moderate — JSON-LD parsing overhead | High for travel/ID; narrow scope |
| **Wallet ecosystem** | Broad (EUDI ARF compatible) | Narrower tooling maturity | Narrow (driver licence use case) |
| **Revocation** | Bitstring Status List (W3C draft) | StatusList2021 | OCSP or embedded validity period |
| **Implementation complexity** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Medium; hardware-binding optional |
| **Degradation path** | QR fallback, paper + cryptographic anchor | QR fallback | QR / NFC fallback |

*Note: CREDEBL SDK v1.0.0 and similar open-source issuance stacks can support SD-JWT VC and W3C VC 2.0 paths. CDPI does not endorse any specific product — these are format-level trade-offs.*

---

## Advice

| Requirement Category | Ubiquitous Standard | Guidance | Considerations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Credential issuance** | OpenID for Verifiable Credential Issuance (OID4VCI) | Civil registry (and education authority) act as anchor issuers; sign credentials with institutional keys; deliver to citizen wallet via OID4VCI authorization code flow | Issuer key management and rotation policy must be defined before launch; HSM custody recommended |
| **Credential presentation** | OpenID for Verifiable Presentations (OID4VP) | Relying parties (employers, banks, border services) verify via OID4VP; challenge-response nonce prevents replay; short nonce expiry (30–60 s) | For in-person offline verification, pair with ISO/IEC 18013-5 QR / NFC flow; cache issuer public keys locally |
| **Selective disclosure** | SD-JWT VC (IETF draft-ietf-oauth-sd-jwt-vc) | Enable citizens to disclose only required claims (e.g., age-over-18 without full DOB) | Validate salt entropy and hash digest matching per OID4VP spec; prevents correlation across presentations |
| **Trust registry** | W3C VC Data Model 2.0 + bilateral trust agreements | Publish issuer DIDs and public keys in a government-operated trust registry; enable cross-border lookup for regional partners (COMESA, EU) | Federated trust avoids single central authority; bilateral agreements must specify accepted credential formats and signature suites explicitly |
| **Revocation** | Bitstring Status List (W3C) / StatusList2021 | Embed status list URL in credential; verifiers check revocation before acceptance; cache status lists for offline contexts | Compromised credentials remain valid until cache expires in offline mode — set validity periods conservatively (e.g., 24 h cache max) |

Seychelles' small population is an advantage: a phased rollout starting with civil registry (birth certificate VC) and education credentials reaches meaningful coverage quickly. The shared rail — issuer registry, trust registry, OID4VCI/VP endpoints — is state-operated on open standards. Wallet applications and relying-party integrations belong in the market layer. The state must not operate or mandate a specific wallet.

---

**Assumptions and refinement**
I assumed Seychelles has no existing national VC or PKI layer in production. I assumed cross-border interoperability with COMESA and EU partners is a near-term requirement given the island economy context. I assumed mobile connectivity is broadly available but that an offline degradation path is still needed for remote islands. If Seychelles has an existing CRVS modernisation programme or an e-government PKI already in procurement, tell me which and I'll revise the issuer architecture section accordingly.
27644a48fa01e3b9.source: I want a technical note on verifiable credentials for seychelles

407ebc70cbbd31c5.title: Verifiable Credentials for Seychelles
407ebc70cbbd31c5.content: --- marp: true theme: cdpi paginate: true --- # Verifiable Credentials for Seychelles ## A standards-based VC layer that works for every resident CDPI · Seychelles · 2025 --- # The Ask - Approve a government-operated VC issuance and trust registry layer - Adopt open standards (OID4VCI, OID4VP, SD-JWT VC) — no proprietary lock-in - Begin with civil registry and education as anchor issuers in Phase 1 - Mandate no single wallet — let the market serve citizens --- # The Outcome - Every Seychelles resident can present a government-issued credential digitally — within 18 months of Phase 1 launch - Cross-border verification with COMESA and EU partners enabled via open trust registry - Paper re-verification cost eliminated for employers, banks, and border services - Selective disclosure: citizens share only what is required — not full records --- # The Gap - Today: residents carry paper proofs; every institution re-verifies from scratch - No national VC issuance layer or trust registry exists in production - Cross-border employment and education verification is slow and manual - No offline fallback for remote island contexts - Small population of ~100,000 means full coverage is achievable fast — if the rail is right --- # Three Principles That Shape This Design - **Inclusion = scale:** design the main channel for every resident; no separate paths by ability or status - **Minimalism produces reach:** every credential field not collected is a citizen not excluded — SD-JWT selective disclosure enforces this - **Measure usage, not issuance:** track presentations-per-issued-credential, not credential count alone --- # How It Works — The Standards Stack OID4VCI OID4VP - **Issuance:** civil registry signs credentials; citizen wallet receives via OID4VCI auth-code flow - **Presentation:** relying parties verify via OID4VP; nonce-based challenge prevents replay - **Selective disclosure:** SD-JWT VC lets citizens prove age-over-18 without exposing full DOB - **Revocation:** Bitstring Status List embedded in each credential; verifiers check before acceptance - **Offline:** ISO 18013-5 QR / NFC fallback; issuer keys pre-cached for remote islands --- # Proof Points **India — Aadhaar eKYC** - 1.4 billion enrolled; authentication APIs now exceed 15 billion calls annually — 15× the enrolment number - Usage, not enrolment, is the metric that proves a credential rail is working **EU — EUDI Architecture Reference Framework** - SD-JWT VC and OID4VP adopted as baseline; Seychelles alignment ensures COMESA + EU cross-border readiness from day one --- # Inclusion Design - **No smartphone requirement on the verifier side** — offline QR / NFC path covers border agents and employers on remote islands - **No single mandated wallet** — market competition drives accessibility; government sets the standard, not the app - **Residents without civil registration:** Phase 1 exclusion audit required before anchor issuer goes live - **Low-literacy contexts:** credential UI must pass plain-language and icon-legibility review - **Revocation awareness:** status list cache max 24 h — compromised credentials cannot persist silently --- # Rollout Proposal **Phase 1 — 0–12 months** - Stand up issuer registry, trust registry, OID4VCI/VP endpoints - Civil registry + education authority as first two issuers - Pilot: 5,000 residents; measure presentations-per-credential at 90 days **Scale trigger:** ≥3 relying parties integrated; presentation success rate ≥95% **Phase 2 — 12–24 months** - Open issuer onboarding to health, employment authorities - Cross-border trust agreements with COMESA and EU partners signed - Rollback condition: credential revocation failure rate >1% triggers architecture review before expansion --- # The Ask — Next Step - **Decide:** approve the OID4VCI / OID4VP / SD-JWT VC stack as the national standard - **Convene:** civil registry, education authority, border services, and one major bank as founding relying party - **Commission:** a 12-week CDPI advisory cycle to produce the Phase 1 implementation strategy note **Contact: info@cdpi.dev** --- ## Assumptions - Assumed audience = Ministry of Information Technology / e-Government authority, Seychelles - Assumed use case = national VC layer anchored at civil registry, with cross-border interoperability - Assumed no existing PKI or VC layer in production - To re-draft, tell me which assumption to change
407ebc70cbbd31c5.source: i want a pitch deck as well

74e936d7e7b8f4b9.title: DaaS Technical Scope Document — Spark Tier — [Country Name]
74e936d7e7b8f4b9.content:
# DaaS Technical Scope Document — Spark Tier — [Country Name]

**Version:** 1.0
**Date:** [Date]
**Prepared for:** [DPI Owner] & Ecosystem Stakeholders
**Tier:** Spark (First DPI Launch, Two Use Cases)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

[Country Name] is launching its first national Verifiable Credential (VC) infrastructure under the DPI as a Packaged Solution (DaaS) model, Spark tier. The programme deploys two end-to-end use cases — [Use Case 1] and [Use Case 2] — within a 16-week active rollout, followed by a 10-week post-rollout support period.

The infrastructure is standards-based (OpenID4VCI, OpenID4VP, W3C VC 2.0, DID Core), government-owned, and designed to serve the full population without exclusion paths. Success is measured by authentications per enrolled citizen, not enrolment count.

**Headline outcomes:**

| Outcome | Target |
|---|---|
| Use cases live end-to-end | 2 |
| Issuers onboarded | 2 |
| Verifiers onboarded | ≥ 2 |
| Active credential holders (end of rollout) | [Target number] |
| Authentications per enrolled citizen (Month 6) | [Target ratio] |

---

## 2. Country Context

| Dimension | Current State | Implication for Design |
|---|---|---|
| Digital ID coverage | [X]% of adult population holds a recognised ID | Enrolment must not require digital ID as prerequisite |
| Connectivity | [Urban/rural split] | Offline verification path required for low-connectivity zones |
| Smartphone penetration | [X]% | Credential wallet must degrade gracefully to USSD or paper QR |
| Existing registries | [List e.g. civil registry, land registry] | Issuance pipeline draws from these; data quality audit required first |
| Policy mandate | [Relevant legislation or ministerial directive] | Legal basis for credential issuance and data sharing confirmed |
| Prior DPI deployments | None (first DPI launch) | Trust-building with citizens and ministries is a primary workstream |

---

## 3. Actors

| Actor | Role | Organisation | Obligations |
|---|---|---|---|
| DPI Owner | Programme authority, funding, policy | [Ministry of ICT / equivalent] | Provide registry access, sign off on milestones, own platform post-rollout |
| Issuer 1 | Issues credentials for Use Case 1 | [Ministry / Agency] | Data quality audit, API or batch integration with credential issuance pipeline |
| Issuer 2 | Issues credentials for Use Case 2 | [Ministry / Agency] | Data quality audit, API or batch integration |
| Verifier(s) | Accept and verify credentials presented by citizens | [List accepting organisations] | Integrate verification SDK or scan QR, train frontline staff |
| Service Provider (SP) | Technical delivery partner | [To be procured] | Deploy, configure, support all components per this scope |
| ISV / DPG Partner | Open-source credentialing software | [To be selected — must be a recognised DPG] | L2/L3 software support, bug fixes, standards compliance |
| CDPI | Technical architecture advisory | CDPI / IIIT Bangalore | Standards review, scope validation, no procurement authority |
| Citizens | Credential holders and presenters | [Country Name] population | Receive, store, and present credentials via wallet |

---

## 4. Components

### 4.1 Shared Rail (Government-Owned, Open Standards Only)

| Component | Function | Standard | Hosted by |
|---|---|---|---|
| Credential Issuance (CI) | Signs and issues W3C VCs to citizens | OpenID4VCI, W3C VC 2.0, DID Core 1.0 | SP on behalf of DPI Owner |
| Credential Store (CS) | Citizen-facing wallet to receive and hold credentials | OpenID4VCI, PKCE, ISO/IEC 18013-5 (mDL) for offline | SP on behalf of DPI Owner |
| Credential Verification (CV) | Verifier-facing SDK and portal to verify presented credentials | OpenID4VP, W3C VC 2.0 | SP on behalf of DPI Owner |
| Trust Registry | Authoritative list of recognised issuers and their public keys | DID Core 1.0, DNS-anchored or ledger-anchored | DPI Owner |
| PKI / Key Management | Signing keys for issuers; revocation list | X.509 or DID-based; OCSP or Status List 2021 | DPI Owner (SP-assisted setup) |

### 4.2 Market Layer (SP / ISV Responsibility)

| Component | Notes |
|---|---|
| Wallet UX and branding | SP delivers; DPI Owner approves design |
| Verifier onboarding portal | SP delivers; must use open standard APIs |
| Notification services (SMS, email) | SP delivers using country-standard channels |
| AI-assisted tooling (Spark tier) | Developer-facing only; no autonomous credential decisions |

---

## 5. Use Cases

### 5.1 Use Case 1 — [Use Case Name, e.g. National Education Certificate]

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Sector | [e.g. Education] |
| Problem addressed | [e.g. Manual certificate verification takes 4–6 weeks and costs USD X per application] |
| Issuer | [e.g. Ministry of Education] |
| Credential type | [e.g. Secondary School Leaving Certificate as W3C VC] |
| Target population | [e.g. 350,000 annual school leavers] |
| Verifiers | [e.g. Universities, employers, immigration authorities] |
| Citizen journey | Citizen receives SMS → opens wallet → downloads signed credential → presents QR to verifier → verifier scans and gets green/red result in under 3 seconds |
| Offline path | QR code on credential scannable without connectivity; verifier device caches trust registry |
| Success criterion | [e.g. 80% of annual school leavers hold a verified digital certificate by end of rollout] |
| Issuer readiness risk | [e.g. Ministry records are partially paper — data digitisation required before issuance pipeline can be built] |

### 5.2 Use Case 2 — [Use Case Name, e.g. Business Registration Credential]

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Sector | [e.g. Commerce / Business Registration] |
| Problem addressed | [e.g. Business owners must visit registry offices in person to prove registration status, costing 2 days and USD Y] |
| Issuer | [e.g. Companies Registry] |
| Credential type | [e.g. Certificate of Incorporation as W3C VC] |
| Target population | [e.g. 120,000 registered businesses] |
| Verifiers | [e.g. Banks, procurement officers, licensing authorities] |
| Citizen journey | Business owner logs into registry portal → requests VC → signs with wallet PIN → presents to bank via QR or deep link → bank verifier confirms status in under 3 seconds |
| Offline path | Signed VC with embedded status check; verifier caches revocation list |
| Success criterion | [e.g. 60% of registered businesses hold and have presented their credential at least once within 6 months] |
| Issuer readiness risk | [e.g. Companies Registry API does not yet expose structured data — batch export integration needed] |

---

## 6. Requirements Matrix

### 6.1 Credential Issuance (CI)

| ID | Requirement | Spark Tier | Standard | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CI-01 | Issue W3C VC 2.0 compliant credentials | Mandatory | W3C VC 2.0 | JSON-LD or CBOR-LD encoding both acceptable |
| CI-02 | Support batch issuance (for existing record holders) | Mandatory | OpenID4VCI | Required for backlog of existing registry records |
| CI-03 | Support on-demand issuance (citizen-initiated) | Mandatory | OpenID4VCI | Triggered via wallet app or USSD fallback |
| CI-04 | Sign credentials with issuer DID and private key | Mandatory | DID Core 1.

verifier caches revocation list | | Success criterion | [e.g. 60% of registered businesses hold and have presented their credential at least once within 6 months] | | Issuer readiness risk | [e.g. Companies Registry API does not yet expose structured data — batch export integration needed] |  ---  ## 6. Requirements Matrix  ### 6.1 Credential Issuance (CI)  | ID | Requirement | Spark Tier | Standard | |---|---|---|---| | CI-01 | Issue W3C VC 2.0 compliant credentials | Mandatory | W3C VC 2.0 | | CI-02 | Support batch issuance (for existing record holders) | Mandatory | OpenID4VCI | | CI-03 | Support on-demand issuance (citizen-initiated) | Mandatory | OpenID4VCI | | CI-04 | Sign credentials with issuer DID and private key | Mandatory | DID Core 1.0, Ed25519 or ECDSA P-256 | | CI-05 | Support credential revocation | Mandatory | Status List 2021 or

aa6c589e27934ac0.title: Technical Scope — Verifiable Credentials Infrastructure, Seychelles
aa6c589e27934ac0.content:
# Technical Scope — Verifiable Credentials Infrastructure, Seychelles

## 1. Purpose

This document defines the technical scope for deploying a government-operated Verifiable Credentials (VC) infrastructure in Seychelles. It covers the shared-rail components the state must operate and the market-layer components the state must not prescribe. Every requirement is traceable to an open standard.

---

## 2. Scope Boundaries

| Layer | Owner | What It Includes | What It Excludes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shared rail** | Government of Seychelles | Issuer registry, trust registry, OID4VCI/VP endpoints, revocation service, national PKI for issuers | Wallet apps, relying-party apps, sector credential schemas beyond anchor use cases |
| **Market layer** | Private sector / agencies | Citizen wallet apps, employer/bank relying-party integrations, sector-specific schema extensions | Any component that requires government exclusivity or proprietary lock-in |

---

## 3. Phase Structure

### Phase 1 — Foundation (Months 0–12)

**Objective:** Stand up the minimum viable shared rail with two anchor issuers.

| Component | Requirement | Standard | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Issuer registry | Government issuers publish DID documents and public keys | W3C DID Core 1.0 | All issuer DIDs resolvable via HTTPS DID method |
| Trust registry | Relying parties query issuer trust status | W3C VC Data Model 2.0 | Trust list publicly readable; update latency ≤ 5 minutes |
| OID4VCI endpoint | Civil registry issues birth certificate VC to citizen wallet | OID4VCI (IETF draft-ietf-oauth-openid4vci) | Authorization code flow completes in ≤ 3 s on 3G |
| OID4VCI endpoint | Education authority issues qualification VC | OID4VCI (IETF draft-ietf-oauth-openid4vci) | Credential schema validated against published JSON schema |
| OID4VP endpoint | Relying parties request and verify presentations | OID4VP (IETF draft-ietf-oauth-openid4vp) | Nonce expiry enforced at ≤ 60 s; replay rejected |
| Selective disclosure | Citizens disclose partial claims | SD-JWT VC (IETF draft-ietf-oauth-sd-jwt-vc) | Age-over-18 proof verifiable without DOB disclosure |
| Revocation service | Issuers revoke credentials | Bitstring Status List (W3C) | Status list refresh ≤ 1 h; verifiers cache max 24 h |
| Offline verification | Border agents and remote-island relying parties verify without connectivity | ISO/IEC 18013-5 QR / NFC | Verification completes offline using cached issuer keys |
| HSM key management | Issuer signing keys protected in hardware | FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or equivalent | Key ceremony documented; rotation policy defined before go-live |

**Phase 1 exit criterion:** ≥ 5,000 resident credentials issued; ≥ 3 relying parties integrated; presentation success rate ≥ 95% at 90-day mark.

---

### Phase 2 — Scale (Months 12–24)

**Objective:** Open issuer onboarding; activate cross-border trust.

| Component | Requirement | Standard | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Issuer onboarding portal | Health, employment, and other authorities self-onboard as issuers | OID4VCI + W3C DID Core 1.0 | Onboarding process documented; new issuer live within 5 business days |
| Cross-border trust agreements | COMESA and EU partners accept Seychelles-issued VCs | Bilateral trust framework referencing W3C VC DM 2.0 | At least one COMESA partner and one EU entry point accept VC presentations |
| Schema registry | Sector schemas published and versioned | JSON Schema draft-07 or later | Schemas publicly accessible; version history retained |
| Monitoring and observability | Presentations-per-issued-credential tracked per issuer | OpenTelemetry (CNCF) | Dashboard live; alert threshold defined for revocation failure rate > 1% |

**Phase 2 exit criterion:** ≥ 5 active issuers; cross-border verification live with ≥ 1 partner; revocation failure rate < 1%.

---

## 4. What the State Operates vs. What the Market Builds

This boundary is load-bearing. The state operating a wallet is equivalent to the state operating a browser — it collapses the market layer and creates lock-in.

| State operates | Market builds |
|---|---|
| Trust registry | Citizen wallet apps (iOS, Android, web) |
| Issuer registry | Employer, bank, and border relying-party apps |
| OID4VCI / OID4VP endpoints | Sector-specific credential UX |
| Revocation service | Wallet-to-wallet sharing flows |
| National PKI for issuers | Accessibility overlays and low-literacy UX |
| Schema registry | Third-party verification SDKs |

---

## 5. Inclusion Requirements

These are non-negotiable. They apply to Phase 1.

| Risk | Mitigation | Owner |
|---|---|---|
| Residents without civil registration cannot receive anchor VC | Phase 1 exclusion audit required before anchor issuer goes live; enrolment gap report to Ministry | Civil Registry + CDPI advisory |
| No smartphone on verifier side | Offline QR / NFC path (ISO 18013-5) must be live at Phase 1 launch — not a Phase 2 add | Shared rail team |
| Low-literacy credential UX | Wallet market-layer apps must pass plain-language and icon-legibility review before relying-party integration | Market layer; government sets review standard |
| Remote island connectivity gaps | Issuer public keys pre-cached on verifier devices; status list max cache 24 h | Shared rail team |

---

## 6. Out of Scope

The following are explicitly out of scope for this engagement:

- Biometric enrolment or liveness detection at the VC layer (belongs in the identity system feeding the civil registry)
- A government-operated citizen wallet
- Sector-specific credential schema design beyond birth certificate and education qualification
- Procurement or vendor selection (escalates to government ICT procurement authority)
- Programme management, stakeholder mapping, or change management

---

## 7. Rollback Conditions

| Trigger | Action |
|---|---|
| Presentation success rate < 90% at 60-day Phase 1 checkpoint | Halt new issuer onboarding; root-cause analysis within 10 business days |
| Revocation failure rate > 1% | Freeze Phase 2 expansion; architecture review before any new relying-party integrations |
| HSM key compromise | Immediate credential revocation for affected issuer; key re-ceremony within 48 h |
| Cross-border partner rejects VC format | Schema and signature suite review against partner's stated standard before re-submission |

---

## 8. Open Questions Requiring Government Decision

1. **DID method selection:** `did:web` (simpler, HTTPS-dependent) vs. `did:ion` or `did:key` (more resilient, higher complexity). Decision required before Phase 1 issuer registry build.
2. **Cloud vs. on-premise hosting:** shared rail components may be hosted on government data centre or sovereign cloud. Decision affects latency SLAs and audit jurisdiction.
3. **Credential schema governance:** who approves new schemas in Phase 2 — a technical committee, the civil registry, or a designated e-government authority?
4. **Wallet accreditation:** will the government publish a conformance test suite for market-layer wallets, or rely on open-standard compliance alone?

---

**Assumptions and refinement**
I assumed Seychelles has no existing VC infrastructure or national PKI in production. I assumed the Ministry of Information Technology (or equivalent) is the DPI owner. I assumed COMESA and EU cross-border interoperability are Phase 2 targets. I assumed biometric enrolment is handled upstream by the civil registry and is not in scope here. If any of these are incorrect — particularly if a PKI or e-government platform is already in procurement — tell me which to change and I will revise the relevant sections.
aa6c589e27934ac0.source: come up with a technical scope doc for this

b1c9858200d23f03.title: Technical Advisory Note — Verifiable Credentials for Uganda's LC1 Community Identity Document
b1c9858200d23f03.content:
# Technical Advisory Note — Verifiable Credentials for Uganda's LC1 Community Identity Document

**Ask:** How should Uganda issue LC1 documents as interoperable verifiable credentials?

**Why:** LC1 credentials issued digitally cut verification time and fraud risk nationally.

**Scope:** Issuance architecture, verification flows, and trust registry for LC1 VCs.

**Context:**
LC1 (Local Council 1) letters are Uganda's most granular community-level identity attestation.
LC1 letters are often the only proof of residence or identity for rural populations.
No universal digital ID enrolment can be assumed; offline verification paths are required.

---

## Options

| Feature | W3C VC 2.0 + OpenID4VCI | PDF with Signed QR (X.509) | Paper + Centralised Registry Lookup |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open standard** | W3C VC 2.0, OpenID4VCI draft 13 | ETSI PAdES / ISO 32000-2 | No credential standard |
| **Offline verification** | Yes — with issuer public key cached | Yes — QR encodes signed payload | No — requires live registry call |
| **Wallet / eLocker storage** | Yes — any OID4VC-compliant wallet | Yes — PDF stored anywhere | No digital artefact |
| **Selective disclosure** | Yes — SD-JWT VC or BBS+ | No | No |
| **Issuer capability required** | Signing key + VC issuance API | Signing key + PDF template renderer | None (paper only) |
| **Degradation path (no smartphone)** | Print signed QR from eLocker | Native format | Native format |
| **Cross-border reuse** | Yes — DID-based issuer resolves globally | Limited — requires X.509 trust chain agreement | None |
| **Revocation** | StatusList2021 or IETF Token Status List | CRL / OCSP | Manual registry update |

---

## Advice

| Requirement Category | Ubiquitous Standard | Guidance | Considerations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Credential format** | W3C VC 2.0 (JSON-LD or SD-JWT VC) | Issue LC1 credentials in W3C VC 2.0 format with a national LC1 schema registered in a public schema registry | SD-JWT VC is lighter for low-bandwidth contexts; JSON-LD is richer for semantic interoperability — choose based on verifier ecosystem maturity |
| **Issuance protocol** | OpenID4VCI (draft 13) | LC1 chairpersons authenticate via OTP or biometric; credential delivered to holder wallet or printed QR | If smartphone penetration is low in the sub-county, prioritise the print-with-signed-QR path as the primary channel, not a fallback |
| **Holder binding and wallet** | ISO/IEC 18013-5 (mDL) device binding or SD-JWT VC cnf claim | Bind credential to holder device or eLocker; allow delegated custody by a household head for dependants | Do not require smartphone ownership as a pre-condition; eLocker cloud custody covers smartphone-absent holders |
| **Verification (offline)** | W3C VC verification + issuer public key cached locally | Verifier apps (police, banks, telcos) cache LC1 issuer public key; verify signature and StatusList2021 offline | Revocation checking is online; define a risk policy for offline revocation tolerance (e.g., 24-hour cache acceptable for low-risk use cases) |
| **Trust registry** | DID (did:web or did:key) for LC1 issuer DIDs | National trust registry lists authorised LC1 chairperson signing keys; keys rotated on chairperson change | Chairperson turnover is frequent — key rotation and revocation of issuer credentials must be operationally simple; automate via parish-level administration portal |

The shared rail is: national schema registry, trust registry of LC1 issuer DIDs, and the revocation status endpoint — all operated by a government anchor (e.g., NIRA or Ministry of Local Government). The market layer is: wallet applications, verifier SDKs, and print/QR rendering services. No proprietary solution belongs in the shared rail.

---

**Assumptions and refinement:**
- I assumed Uganda has no existing national VC trust registry; if NIRA or another body already operates a PKI or DID registry, the trust anchor design changes — tell me and I'll redraft Section 6.
- I assumed LC1 chairpersons have intermittent internet access for issuance but verifiers may be fully offline — if connectivity is better or worse, the offline revocation tolerance guidance in row 4 should be adjusted.
- I assumed the primary use cases are residence proof and identity attestation for financial services and government services (consistent with CDPI's Uganda education credentials engagement). If the primary use case is cross-border mobility, the ISO/IEC 18013-5 mDL path deserves a dedicated note.
- "Uganda" is used throughout; if this is being piloted in a specific district before national rollout, scope the trust registry to that district's issuer DIDs first and federate upward.
b1c9858200d23f03.source: I want a technical note on verifiable credentials for Uganda and their LC1 document

bd377b0f26391ca2.title: DaaS Technical Scope Document — Spark Tier — [Country Name]
bd377b0f26391ca2.content:
# DaaS Technical Scope Document — Spark Tier — [Country Name]

**Version:** 1.0
**Date:** [Date]
**Prepared for:** [DPI Owner] & Ecosystem Stakeholders
**Tier:** Spark (First DPI Launch, Two Use Cases)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

[Country Name] is launching its first national Verifiable Credential (VC) infrastructure under the DPI as a Packaged Solution (DaaS) model, Spark tier. The programme deploys two end-to-end use cases — [Use Case 1] and [Use Case 2] — within a 16-week active rollout, followed by a 10-week post-rollout support period.

The infrastructure is standards-based (OpenID4VCI, OpenID4VP, W3C VC 2.0, DID Core), government-owned, and designed to serve the full population without exclusion paths. Success is measured by authentications per enrolled citizen, not enrolment count.

**Headline outcomes:**
| Outcome | Target |
|---|---|
| Use cases live end-to-end | 2 |
| Issuers onboarded | 2 |
| Verifiers onboarded | ≥ 2 |
| Active credential holders (end of rollout) | [Target number] |
| Authentications per enrolled citizen (Month 6) | [Target ratio] |

---

## 2. Country Context

| Dimension | Current State | Implication for Design |
|---|---|---|
| Digital ID coverage | [X]% of adult population holds a recognised ID | Enrolment must not require digital ID as prerequisite |
| Connectivity | [Urban/rural split] | Offline verification path required for low-connectivity zones |
| Smartphone penetration | [X]% | Credential wallet must degrade gracefully to USSD or paper QR |
| Existing registries | [List e.g. civil registry, land registry] | Issuance pipeline draws from these; data quality audit required first |
| Policy mandate | [Relevant legislation or ministerial directive] | Legal basis for credential issuance and data sharing confirmed |
| Prior DPI deployments | None (first DPI launch) | Trust-building with citizens and ministries is a primary workstream |

---

## 3. Actors

| Actor | Role | Organisation | Obligations |
|---|---|---|---|
| DPI Owner | Programme authority, funding, policy | [Ministry of ICT / equivalent] | Provide registry access, sign off on milestones, own platform post-rollout |
| Issuer 1 | Issues credentials for Use Case 1 | [Ministry / Agency] | Data quality audit, API or batch integration with credential issuance pipeline |
| Issuer 2 | Issues credentials for Use Case 2 | [Ministry / Agency] | Data quality audit, API or batch integration |
| Verifier(s) | Accept and verify credentials presented by citizens | [List accepting organisations] | Integrate verification SDK or scan QR, train frontline staff |
| Service Provider (SP) | Technical delivery partner | [To be procured] | Deploy, configure, support all components per this scope |
| ISV / DPG Partner | Open-source credentialing software | [To be selected — must be a recognised DPG] | L2/L3 software support, bug fixes, standards compliance |
| CDPI | Technical architecture advisory | CDPI / IIIT Bangalore | Standards review, scope validation, no procurement authority |
| Citizens | Credential holders and presenters | [Country Name] population | Receive, store, and present credentials via wallet |

---

## 4. Components

### 4.1 Shared Rail (Government-Owned, Open Standards Only)

| Component | Function | Standard | Hosted by |
|---|---|---|---|
| Credential Issuance (CI) | Signs and issues W3C VCs to citizens | OpenID4VCI, W3C VC 2.0, DID Core 1.0 | SP on behalf of DPI Owner |
| Credential Store (CS) | Citizen-facing wallet to receive and hold credentials | OpenID4VCI, PKCE, ISO/IEC 18013-5 (mDL) for offline | SP on behalf of DPI Owner |
| Credential Verification (CV) | Verifier-facing SDK and portal to verify presented credentials | OpenID4VP, W3C VC 2.0 | SP on behalf of DPI Owner |
| Trust Registry | Authoritative list of recognised issuers and their public keys | DID Core 1.0, DNS-anchored or ledger-anchored | DPI Owner |
| PKI / Key Management | Signing keys for issuers; revocation list | X.509 or DID-based; OCSP or Status List 2021 | DPI Owner (SP-assisted setup) |

### 4.2 Market Layer (SP / ISV Responsibility)

| Component | Notes |
|---|---|
| Wallet UX and branding | SP delivers; DPI Owner approves design |
| Verifier onboarding portal | SP delivers; must use open standard APIs |
| Notification services (SMS, email) | SP delivers using country-standard channels |
| AI-assisted tooling (Spark tier) | Developer-facing only; no autonomous credential decisions |

---

## 5. Use Cases

### 5.1 Use Case 1 — [Use Case Name, e.g. National Education Certificate]

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Sector | [e.g. Education] |
| Problem addressed | [e.g. Manual certificate verification takes 4–6 weeks and costs USD X per application] |
| Issuer | [e.g. Ministry of Education] |
| Credential type | [e.g. Secondary School Leaving Certificate as W3C VC] |
| Target population | [e.g. 350,000 annual school leavers] |
| Verifiers | [e.g. Universities, employers, immigration authorities] |
| Citizen journey | Citizen receives SMS → opens wallet → downloads signed credential → presents QR to verifier → verifier scans and gets green/red result in under 3 seconds |
| Offline path | QR code on credential scannable without connectivity; verifier device caches trust registry |
| Success criterion | [e.g. 80% of annual school leavers hold a verified digital certificate by end of rollout] |
| Issuer readiness risk | [e.g. Ministry records are partially paper — data digitisation required before issuance pipeline can be built] |

### 5.2 Use Case 2 — [Use Case Name, e.g. Business Registration Credential]

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Sector | [e.g. Commerce / Business Registration] |
| Problem addressed | [e.g. Business owners must visit registry offices in person to prove registration status, costing 2 days and USD Y] |
| Issuer | [e.g. Companies Registry] |
| Credential type | [e.g. Certificate of Incorporation as W3C VC] |
| Target population | [e.g. 120,000 registered businesses] |
| Verifiers | [e.g. Banks, procurement officers, licensing authorities] |
| Citizen journey | Business owner logs into registry portal → requests VC → signs with wallet PIN → presents to bank via QR or deep link → bank verifier confirms status in under 3 seconds |
| Offline path | Signed VC with embedded status check; verifier caches revocation list |
| Success criterion | [e.g. 60% of registered businesses hold and have presented their credential at least once within 6 months] |
| Issuer readiness risk | [e.g. Companies Registry API does not yet expose structured data — batch export integration needed] |

---

## 6. Requirements Matrix

### 6.1 Credential Issuance (CI)

| ID | Requirement | Spark Tier | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| CI-01 | Issue W3C VC 2.0 compliant credentials | Mandatory | W3C VC 2.0 |
| CI-02 | Support batch issuance (for existing record holders) | Mandatory | OpenID4VCI |
| CI-03 | Support on-demand issuance (citizen-initiated) | Mandatory | OpenID4VCI |
| CI-04 | Sign credentials with issuer DID and private key | Mandatory | DID Core 1.0, Ed25519 or ECDSA P-256 |
| CI-05 | Support credential revocation | Mandatory | Status List 2021 or
bd377b0f26391ca2.source: Draft a DaaS Technical Scope Document (Spark tier) for a first DPI launch with two use cases. Include Executive Summary, Country Context, Actors table, Components, Use Cases, Requirements Matrix, Deliverables, and Governance sections.

daeb7f826a29979a.title: A Pitch Deck
daeb7f826a29979a.content:
---
marp: true
theme: cdpi
paginate: true
---

<!-- _class: title -->

# A Pitch Deck

## [Audience] · [Ministry / Agency] · [Country]

CDPI · [Country] · [Date]

---

# The Ask

- Tell us what you want built, and we can build the deck around it
- Share the country, the ministry, the use case, and the target population
- Example: "Pitch deck for Kenya's Ministry of Health to adopt a health wallet using verifiable credentials"

---

# What We Need From You

- **Country** — which government are we advising?
- **Use case** — identity, payments, data sharing, verifiable credentials?
- **Audience** — minister, CIO, inter-departmental, donor?
- **Population** — who must this reach, and who is currently excluded?
- **Phase** — is this a new adoption pitch, an inter-departmental pitch, or a rollout plan?

---

# Why It Matters

- Every slide should map to a real population outcome
- The deck structure changes depending on whether you're pitching adoption, scale, or inclusion
- Give us the context and we produce a finished, presenter-ready deck — not an outline

---

# The Ask (Close)

- Share the details above in your next message
- We will produce a full, branded, Marp-ready deck in one turn
- No outlines, no placeholders — a deck you can present

---

## Assumptions

- No use case, country, ministry, or audience was provided in this request.
- To re-draft: reply with country, use case, audience, and target population.
- Every field you give us narrows the exclusion risk in the deck itself.
daeb7f826a29979a.source: I want a pitch deck for this:
